result:
The silence of an empty page is not truly silent. It hums with a frequency just below hearing—the collective potential of every story not yet told, every argument not yet made, every confession still coiled tight in the chest. It is the sound of the subjunctive mood, the world of *what if* and *could be*. And here, in this allotted space of a thousand words, we step into that hum to build a small, temporary shelter of meaning.

I find myself thinking of lichen. Not a grand subject, perhaps, but a true one. It is not one thing, but two: a fungus and an algae (or sometimes a bacterium), living in such intimate entanglement that they present as a single organism. The fungus provides structure, a foothold on bare rock. The algae, through photosynthesis, provides food. Together, they can live where neither could alone—on sun-scorched stone, on wind-lashed Arctic tundra, on the slow-sloping roof of a forgotten shed. They are a pact. They are a lesson in collaboration so fundamental it becomes a new being. We could learn from lichen. Our own societies, so often framed as competitions of singular entities, might better see themselves as symbiotic networks. The artist needs the farmer, the engineer needs the poet, the dreamer needs the skeptic. Our progress is less a ladder and more a mycelial mat, spreading unseen, connecting, sustaining.

This thought leads me, inevitably, to time. We experience it as a river, flowing inexorably forward, carrying us from source to sea. But what if that’s just the limitations of our perception? What if time is more like lichen—a symbiosis of moments? The past is not a sealed trunk but the fungal network, the structure holding everything up. The present is the photosynthetic burst, the vibrant, living *now* that draws energy from light and air. And the future is the spreading form, the shape neither partner could predict alone. The child you were is not gone; it is the structure upon which your current self is built. The grief you carry is not a fossil; it is part of the living organism, changing its color and texture but still essential to the whole.

We spend so much of our lives trying to *point*—to indicate, to explain, to justify. We point to credentials, to possessions, to milestones. "See?" we say. "I am here because of that. My value is proven by this." But what if the pointing itself is the distraction? The lichen does not point. It simply *is*. It asserts its quiet, persistent existence by the very fact of its thriving in a hostile place. Its proof is not in an argument, but in its orange and gray and greenish crust on the world. Perhaps our deepest moments are not the pointed-to ones, but the ones we simply inhabit: the warmth of a sunbeam on a forearm, the shared silence with a loved one that needs no filling, the complete absorption in a task where the self vanishes and only the doing remains. In those moments, we stop narrating our lives and finally live them.

Of course, this is hard. We are haunted by narratives—the ones we’re born into, the ones we craft for ourselves, the ones shouted at us from screens. We are told we must be *legible*: to the economy, to the algorithm, to the social sphere. Legibility requires simplification. It requires turning the complex, symbiotic lichen of a person into a single, labeled datum: Consumer. Voter. Demographic. Diagnosis. But a human being is an ecosystem. We contain forgotten memories like dormant seeds, inherited traumas and talents like ancient geological layers, and sparks of future possibility like undiscovered constellations. To make ourselves legible is often to do violence to that inner ecosystem.

So, what to do? Perhaps we cultivate small pockets of illegibility. A handwritten letter. A garden planted not for beauty but for the strange joy of watching things grow. A poem written and never shared. A song hummed to a sleeping pet. A walk with no destination. These are acts of psychic lichenization. They are quiet collaborations between different parts of the self, creating something hardy and alive on the bare rock of routine and obligation. They don’t need to point to anything. Their value is in their existence.

And this brings me to the thousand words. They are a finite resource, like a breath, or a day. I could use them to build a perfect, polished argument—a miniature cathedral of thought. But maybe it’s better to use them as a wanderer uses a path: not just to get to the end, but to see what lies along the way. The lichen, the nature of time, the tyranny of narrative—these are not waypoints on a linear journey to a conclusion. They are patches of life found on the shared rock of our current moment. They connect because all things, looked at with enough attention, connect.

We are all writing on a limit. Not a thousand words, but a lifespan. A heartbeat count. The terrifying, beautiful finitude is what makes any of it matter. The lichen will outlive me. The rock will outlive the lichen. The slow dance of geology and time will grind it all to dust, and from that dust new life will form, new symbioses will begin. This is not a tragedy; it is the engine of becoming.

My thousand words are nearly spent. Their hum is fading back into the general silence. If there is a point to this wandering—and I’m not sure there needs to be—it is simply this: You are more lichen than lone monument. You are a collaboration across time, a tapestry of inherited and chosen selves. Your value is not in what you point to, but in the quiet, persistent, complex fact of your being. Tend to your symbioses—with others, with your past, with the fragile world. Grow where you are planted, even if the surface seems too hard. And every now and then, stop pointing. Just be. A soft, resilient, miraculous crust of life on the strange and wonderful rock of existence.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
